Keywords: tab bar, bottom nav, navigation, tabs, primary navigation, mobile nav
Description: Primary navigation bar at the bottom of the screen with 3-5 tabs. Each tab leads to a top-level destination. The currently selected tab is visually highlighted.
When to Use: Apps with 3-5 equally important top-level sections. Most consumer and productivity apps. Standard for iOS (Tab Bar) and Android (Bottom Navigation).
PRD Guidance: PRD must specify: number of tabs (3-5), tab labels and icons, which tab is default on launch, badge indicators (e.g., unread count on notifications tab). Each tab should represent a distinct category, not a sequential flow.
Good Example: Instagram: Home, Search, Reels, Shop, Profile — 5 clear top-level destinations with distinct icons and labels
Bad Example: Hamburger menu with 15 items hidden behind a ≡ icon — users don't discover features they can't see
Accessibility Note: All tab items must have text labels (not icon-only). VoiceOver/TalkBack must announce tab name and selected state.

Keywords: refresh, pull down, reload, update, sync, fetch latest
Description: User pulls down on a scrollable list to trigger a data refresh. A spinner or animation indicates loading. Content updates in place when refresh completes.
When to Use: Any screen showing dynamic content that may have changed since last viewed: feeds, inboxes, dashboards, lists. Expected behavior for all list-based mobile UIs.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: what data is refreshed, maximum refresh duration (timeout), loading indicator style, behavior if refresh fails (show error + keep stale data), whether background refresh supplements pull-to-refresh.
Good Example: Twitter/X: Pull down on timeline → spinner → new tweets inserted at top with smooth animation. Content below is preserved.
Bad Example: Forcing a full page reload that loses scroll position and shows a blank screen during loading.
Accessibility Note: Announce 'Refreshing' and 'Refresh complete' to screen readers via aria-live. The pull gesture must have an alternative (refresh button in nav bar).

Keywords: bottom sheet, action sheet, modal, drawer, options, menu, half sheet
Description: A panel that slides up from the bottom of the screen, showing additional options, filters, or content. Can be half-height (peek) or full-height. Dismissed by swiping down or tapping outside.
When to Use: Presenting secondary actions, filters, settings, or confirmations without navigating away from the current screen. Replaces desktop dropdowns and modals on mobile.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: content of the sheet, whether it's dismissible (swipe down, tap outside), whether it blocks interaction with content behind it (modal vs non-modal), default height (peek vs full), snap points for dragging.
Good Example: Google Maps: Tap a location → bottom sheet slides up showing details, reviews, directions. Swipe up for full detail, swipe down to dismiss.
Bad Example: Full-screen modal for a simple 3-option menu. Desktop-style dropdown that's too small to tap accurately on mobile.
Accessibility Note: Bottom sheet must be reachable by VoiceOver/TalkBack. Focus should move to the sheet when it opens. Escape/back gesture must dismiss it.

Keywords: skeleton, shimmer, placeholder, loading state, content loading, progressive
Description: Gray placeholder shapes that mirror the layout of expected content, with a shimmer animation. Replaces traditional spinners for content-heavy screens.
When to Use: Any screen that loads data from an API and displays structured content (cards, lists, profiles). Provides perceived performance improvement — users feel the app is faster even if load time is identical.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which screens use skeleton loading (vs spinner), skeleton layout matching final content structure, animation style (shimmer vs pulse), timeout behavior (show error state after N seconds), transition from skeleton to real content (fade-in).
Good Example: LinkedIn: Profile page shows gray shapes matching avatar, name, title, and post cards while loading. Content fades in smoothly as data arrives.
Bad Example: Blank white screen with a centered spinner for 3 seconds before content appears. Or: spinner in one corner while most of the screen is empty.
Accessibility Note: Skeleton elements should be marked as aria-hidden. Screen reader should announce 'Loading content' and 'Content loaded' when data arrives.

Keywords: infinite scroll, pagination, lazy load, load more, endless scroll, feed, list
Description: Content loads automatically as the user scrolls near the bottom. No 'Next Page' button. New items are appended seamlessly.
When to Use: Feeds, search results, product catalogs, any list with >20 items. Standard for social media, e-commerce, and content apps.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: trigger point (load more when user is N items from bottom), batch size (20-50 items), loading indicator at bottom of list, end-of-list indicator ('No more items'), error state if load fails (retry button), scroll position preservation on back navigation.
Good Example: Instagram: Scrolling the feed seamlessly loads more posts. A small spinner appears briefly at the bottom. User never sees a 'page 2' button.
Bad Example: 'Load More' button at the bottom of every 10-item page. Or: loading all 500 items at once, causing a 10-second initial load and janky scrolling.
Accessibility Note: Announce 'Loading more items' to screen readers when new batch starts loading. New items should be navigable immediately after loading.

Keywords: swipe, swipe to delete, swipe actions, quick actions, list gesture, inbox
Description: Swiping left or right on a list item reveals action buttons (delete, archive, flag, etc.). Common in mail, messaging, and task management apps.
When to Use: Lists where users frequently need to perform quick actions on individual items without opening the detail view. Must always have an alternative tap-based path.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: swipe direction(s) and their actions (left = delete, right = archive), visual treatment of revealed buttons, destructive action confirmation (undo toast or confirm dialog), swipe threshold before action triggers, haptic feedback at threshold.
Good Example: Apple Mail: Swipe left reveals 'Trash' (red) and 'Flag' (orange). Swipe right reveals 'Unread' (blue). Full swipe triggers default action. Undo available via toast.
Bad Example: No swipe actions on any list items — user must tap into each item, find the action, perform it, and navigate back. 3 taps instead of 1 swipe.
Accessibility Note: Swipe actions must have equivalent tap-based alternatives (long-press menu or detail screen actions). VoiceOver users cannot perform custom swipe gestures.

Keywords: fab, floating button, create, add, primary action, compose, new
Description: A prominent circular button floating above content, typically in the bottom-right corner. Represents the single most important action on the screen (create, compose, add).
When to Use: Android apps following Material Design. Use when there is one clear primary creation action per screen. Less common on iOS (where primary actions go in the nav bar).
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which screens show the FAB, what action it triggers, whether it expands into a speed dial (multiple sub-actions), scroll behavior (hide on scroll down, show on scroll up), position (bottom-right default).
Good Example: Gmail: Red 'Compose' FAB on the inbox screen. Tapping opens new email. FAB hides when scrolling down to maximize reading space.
Bad Example: FAB on every screen (even ones with no creation action). Multiple FABs on one screen. FAB that obscures important content at the bottom of the screen.
Accessibility Note: FAB must have an accessible label ('New message', not just '+'). Focus order should include the FAB. Size must be ≥56dp (Android) / 44pt (iOS).

Keywords: empty state, zero state, no data, first run, blank screen, onboarding, first use
Description: What the user sees when a screen has no content yet: no items in a list, no messages, no search results. Must communicate what the screen is for and guide the user to their first action.
When to Use: Every screen that can be empty: new account (no data), search with no results, empty inbox, cleared notifications. The empty state is often the user's FIRST experience with a feature.
PRD Guidance: PRD must specify: empty state for every list/feed/dashboard screen. Each empty state needs: (1) illustration or icon, (2) clear headline explaining what will appear here, (3) CTA button for the primary action to populate it. Never show a completely blank screen.
Good Example: Slack: Empty channel shows: 'This is the very beginning of the #channel-name channel' with a message input ready. Clear, inviting, actionable.
Bad Example: Blank white screen with no text, no CTA, no indication of what this screen does or how to populate it. User doesn't know if the app is broken or empty.
Accessibility Note: Empty state text must be readable by screen readers. CTA button must be focusable. Illustration should have a descriptive alt text.

Keywords: toast, snackbar, inline notification, feedback, success message, undo, confirmation
Description: A brief message that appears at the bottom (Android snackbar) or top (iOS toast) of the screen for 3-5 seconds. Used for lightweight feedback: action confirmation, undo, errors. Non-blocking — user can continue interacting.
When to Use: Confirming non-critical actions (item saved, message sent, setting updated), offering undo for destructive actions, showing temporary errors. NOT for critical information that requires user action.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which actions trigger a toast, message copy, duration (3-5s), whether it includes an action button (e.g., 'Undo'), position (top vs bottom), behavior when multiple toasts are queued (stack vs replace).
Good Example: Gmail: After archiving an email, a snackbar appears at the bottom: 'Conversation archived' with an 'Undo' button. Disappears after 5 seconds.
Bad Example: Full-screen alert dialog for 'Settings saved successfully' requiring the user to tap 'OK' to dismiss. Blocking the user for trivial confirmations.
Accessibility Note: Toast must be announced to screen readers (aria-live='polite'). Duration should be long enough for screen reader users to hear the message (minimum 5 seconds with action button).

Keywords: search, filter, sort, faceted search, refinement, category filter, mobile search
Description: Search bar with filter/sort options, typically presented via bottom sheet or horizontal chip bar. Autocomplete suggestions appear as user types. Recent searches shown on focus.
When to Use: Any app with >50 items of browsable content: e-commerce, marketplaces, content apps, directories. Search is often the fastest path to value on mobile.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: search bar placement (prominent: top of screen or bottom tab), autocomplete behavior (trigger after 2+ chars, latency <200ms), filter categories and values, sort options, recent search history (persist locally), empty results state with suggestions.
Good Example: Airbnb: Search bar at top. Tapping opens full-screen search with suggestions. Filters presented as horizontal chips (Price, Type, Rooms). Filter detail via bottom sheet.
Bad Example: Tiny search icon in a corner. No autocomplete. No recent searches. Filters on a separate page requiring navigation away from results.
Accessibility Note: Search field must have a clear accessible label. Filter changes should announce 'X results found' to screen readers. Voice search should be available as an alternative input method.

Keywords: onboarding, walkthrough, tutorial, carousel, intro screens, first run, welcome
Description: A series of 3-5 swipeable screens shown on first launch, each highlighting a key feature or value proposition. Ends with a CTA to get started.
When to Use: First app launch for new users. Must be skippable. Should convey value, not instructions. Best when each screen has one clear message with an illustration.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: number of screens (3-5 max), content per screen (headline + subtext + illustration), skip button always visible, progress indicator (dots), final CTA ('Get Started'), whether to show again if user reinstalls (no — use backend flag).
Good Example: Duolingo: 3 screens showing core value (learn a language), social proof (500M+ learners), and personalization (choose your language). Takes <30 seconds.
Bad Example: 10-screen tutorial explaining every button in the app. No skip button. Each screen has 200+ words of text. User must swipe through all to access the app.
Accessibility Note: Carousel must be navigable via VoiceOver swipe gestures. Page indicator dots need accessible labels ('Page 1 of 3'). Skip button must be easily discoverable.

Keywords: coach mark, tooltip, spotlight, feature discovery, hint, guided tour, new feature
Description: Overlay highlighting a specific UI element with a brief tooltip explaining its purpose. Shown once per user for new features. Dismissed by tapping anywhere.
When to Use: Introducing a new feature to existing users. Drawing attention to a feature that's hard to discover. Preferable to full walkthroughs for incremental feature education.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which elements get coach marks, copy for each tooltip (<20 words), trigger condition (first time user sees the screen), dismissal behavior (tap anywhere or 'Got it' button), persistence (show once, never again per user).
Good Example: Spotify: When podcasts launched, a coach mark highlighted the Podcasts tab with 'New! Browse and listen to podcasts here' — shown once, dismissed on tap.
Bad Example: 12 coach marks appearing in sequence, blocking the entire app. Or: coach marks that reappear every time the user visits the screen.
Accessibility Note: Coach mark overlay must not trap VoiceOver focus. Tooltip text must be announced. Dismiss button must be focusable.

Keywords: haptic, vibration, feedback, tactile, touch feedback, force touch, 3d touch
Description: Subtle physical feedback (vibration) confirming user actions: successful completion, errors, selection changes, threshold crossings. Uses the device's haptic engine.
When to Use: Confirming non-visual actions (toggle switch, pull-to-refresh threshold, swipe completion), communicating errors, enhancing gesture feedback. Use sparingly — overuse desensitizes users.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which actions trigger haptic feedback, feedback type (light/medium/heavy impact, success, error, selection), platform-specific implementation (UIFeedbackGenerator on iOS, HapticFeedbackConstants on Android). Include in acceptance criteria for gesture-based stories.
Good Example: Apple Pay: Success haptic after payment confirmation. Toggle switches give a light impact haptic. Pull-to-refresh triggers at the release threshold.
Bad Example: Haptic on every button tap (desensitizes the user). Or: no haptic feedback at all on gesture-based interactions where visual confirmation is insufficient.
Accessibility Note: Haptic feedback supplements but does not replace visual and auditory feedback. Users with motor impairments may have device haptics disabled.

Keywords: layout, grid, responsive, adaptive, size class, orientation, tablet, foldable, compact, regular
Description: Layout system that adapts to different screen sizes, orientations, and device form factors. Uses relative sizing, constraint-based layout, and breakpoints rather than fixed pixel dimensions.
When to Use: All mobile apps. Must handle: different phone sizes (SE to Pro Max), orientation changes, iPad/tablet, foldable devices, Dynamic Type scaling. Minimum: portrait phone optimization.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: supported orientations (portrait-only for V1 is acceptable), layout behavior for key screens on different size classes (compact phone, regular tablet), whether landscape is supported, text scaling support (Dynamic Type on iOS, font scaling on Android).
Good Example: Twitter/X: Same app works on iPhone SE, iPhone 15 Pro Max, iPad (with sidebar navigation), and in both orientations. Content reflows gracefully.
Bad Example: Fixed-width layout that looks tiny on a large phone and requires horizontal scrolling on a small phone. Or: portrait-only app that shows a blank screen in landscape.
Accessibility Note: Dynamic Type / font scaling must be supported. Layout must not break when system font size is set to maximum. Touch targets must remain ≥44pt even at large text sizes.

Keywords: dark mode, dark theme, night mode, appearance, light mode, color scheme, dynamic color
Description: Full support for system-wide dark mode preference. All screens adapt colors when the user's device is set to dark mode. Uses semantic colors that respond to appearance changes.
When to Use: All apps targeting iOS 13+ and Android 10+. Dark mode is expected by users (50%+ of mobile users use dark mode). Apple and Google recommend supporting it.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: dark mode support as a non-functional requirement. Use semantic/dynamic colors in design system. Test all screens in both modes. Handle images and illustrations (ensure they work on dark backgrounds). Support manual toggle in app settings in addition to following system setting.
Good Example: Apple Music: Seamless dark mode. All text, backgrounds, and images adapt. Album artwork looks great on both dark and light backgrounds.
Bad Example: Inverted colors that make text unreadable. White text on light gray backgrounds in dark mode. Images with white backgrounds that look jarring.
Accessibility Note: Ensure sufficient contrast ratios (4.5:1) in BOTH light and dark modes. Some users use dark mode for visual accessibility — poor dark mode contrast defeats the purpose.

Keywords: shortcut, quick action, 3d touch, long press icon, app shortcut, static shortcut, dynamic shortcut
Description: Actions available from the app icon via long-press (iOS) or long-press + suggested actions (Android). Provides direct paths to key features without opening the app first.
When to Use: Apps with 2-4 frequently used actions: compose message, start workout, scan QR code, new note. Reduces time-to-action for returning users.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which actions are available as shortcuts (2-4 max), shortcut labels and icons, whether shortcuts are static (always available) or dynamic (based on user behavior), deep link destination for each shortcut.
Good Example: WhatsApp: Long-press icon shows 'New Chat', 'Search', 'QR Code', 'Camera'. Each opens directly to that feature, saving 2-3 taps.
Bad Example: No shortcuts at all (missed opportunity). Or: 8 shortcuts listed — too many defeats the purpose of quick access.
Accessibility Note: Shortcuts must have descriptive labels readable by VoiceOver/TalkBack. Icons should be recognizable at small size.

Keywords: biometric, face id, touch id, fingerprint, secure screen, sensitive data, privacy screen
Description: Using biometric authentication to protect specific screens or actions within the app, beyond initial app unlock. Protects sensitive data if the phone is unlocked and someone else has access.
When to Use: Banking apps (account details, transfers), health apps (medical records), enterprise apps (confidential documents), note apps (private notes). Any screen showing data the user might want to protect even on an unlocked device.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which screens or actions require biometric verification, session timeout (re-verify after N minutes of inactivity), fallback method (PIN/password), behavior on app backgrounding (show privacy screen / blur), behavior if biometric fails N times.
Good Example: Signal: Biometric lock option for the entire app. When enabled, a privacy screen (blurred) is shown in the app switcher. Biometric required every time the app is opened.
Bad Example: Showing all sensitive data without any protection. Or: requiring biometric for every single tap (too aggressive, kills usability).
Accessibility Note: Biometric prompts must announce their purpose to screen readers. Fallback PIN/password must be accessible. Privacy screen in app switcher should not block accessibility features.

Keywords: progressive disclosure, show more, expand, collapse, detail, summary, advanced options
Description: Showing only essential information initially, with the option to reveal more detail on demand. Reduces cognitive load on small screens.
When to Use: Any screen with >5 data points or settings. Complex forms, product detail pages, settings screens. Essential for mobile where screen space is limited.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which information is shown by default (primary) vs hidden (secondary), interaction to reveal more (tap 'Show More', expand section, navigate to detail), how much content to show before truncation, whether expanded state is remembered.
Good Example: Amazon: Product page shows title, price, rating, and delivery date above the fold. Description, specs, reviews are in expandable sections below — user taps to expand what they care about.
Bad Example: All 50 product attributes shown in a long scrolling page with no hierarchy. Or: critical information hidden behind 3 levels of navigation.
Accessibility Note: Expandable sections must be operable via VoiceOver/TalkBack. State changes ('expanded'/'collapsed') must be announced. Hidden content must be accessible when revealed.

Keywords: error, failure, retry, connection error, server error, timeout, something went wrong
Description: How the app communicates errors to users. Must be clear (what went wrong), helpful (what to do about it), and non-technical (no error codes or stack traces in user-facing messages).
When to Use: Every API call, form submission, file upload, sync operation, and network-dependent action. Every feature needs an error state designed explicitly.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: error message copy for each failure scenario (network, server, validation, permission), retry behavior (auto-retry vs manual button), error persistence (inline vs full-screen), whether to preserve user input during form errors, logging of error events for analytics.
Good Example: Slack: 'Couldn't send your message. Tap to retry.' Shown inline below the message. Original text preserved. Retry on tap. Automatic retry in background.
Bad Example: 'Error 500: Internal Server Error' — or worse, a blank screen with no indication that something went wrong. User doesn't know if the action succeeded or failed.
Accessibility Note: Error messages must be announced to screen readers immediately (aria-live='assertive'). Retry button must be focusable. Don't use only red color to indicate errors.

Keywords: gesture nav, edge swipe, back gesture, swipe back, bottom bar, navigation gesture
Description: Modern mobile navigation using swipe gestures instead of (or in addition to) button taps. iOS: edge swipe to go back. Android: bottom gesture bar (swipe up for home, swipe from edge for back).
When to Use: All apps must support platform-standard navigation gestures. Custom gestures should supplement, not replace, standard navigation. Critical for apps with multi-level navigation hierarchies.
PRD Guidance: PRD should specify: which screens support edge swipe back (all by default on iOS), full-screen swipe areas that might conflict with edge gestures (carousels, maps, drawing canvases), how to handle gesture conflicts (disable edge gesture on specific screens if needed, with explicit justification).
Good Example: Apple Maps: Supports standard back gesture everywhere except when in full-screen panning mode. Clear modal presentations use swipe-down to dismiss.
Bad Example: Disabling the system back gesture app-wide to implement custom navigation. Or: custom gesture that conflicts with the system back gesture, making it impossible to go back.
Accessibility Note: All gesture-based navigation must have an equivalent button/tap alternative. VoiceOver/TalkBack users navigate via accessibility actions, not gestures.
